title: Water In A Well
original_prose:
"You can pour water on a drownin’ man

You can try and light a fire with soaking hands

Go on and wish me well

But if you go, don’t put water in a well."

— Sturgill Simpson, “Water in a Well”

####

[SYNRGY PRIORITY BULLETIN – LOCALIZED INSURGENCY SUPPRESSION]ORIGIN: REGIONAL FIDELITY COMMAND – TEXAS GULF ZONE

Civilian unrest in DROWNED ZONE 23B remains elevated.Unsecured broadcast nodes detected near former Galveston coastline.Graffiti, Signal drift, and Spark siphoning increasing.Initiate pre-conversion monitoring of youth clusters.Authorized response includes:• Suspicion-based detainment• Spark audit• Preemptive vessel correction

Contingency Note:Should rebel activity persist beyond next Signal cycle, authorization is requested for tactical NEUROGENIC FISSION DEVICE deployment.Projected casualty yield: 92.4% (noncompliant class).Survivor reclamation rate: negligible.Spiritual impact: favorable.

Obey early. Obey completely. Obey with joy.



####

The house creaked with the tide.

Not the waves. Not anymore. Just the memory of them—lifting the stilts a hair each time the wind shifted, like even the bones of the house wanted to give up.

I woke with salt in my mouth. Not from sweat. From the walls. They wept at night. Left crusted streaks on the ceiling and my skin. Mae was already up, sitting cross-legged near the rusted mirror, brushing out her hair with the back end of a broken fork. She hummed as she worked. Not a song, exactly—just that droning chant they played over the radios. The Signal. Sanitized and slow, like a lullaby for machines.

I sat up. Water dripped somewhere behind me. The house always leaked from somewhere new. Mold bloomed along the edges of the window, curling the wallpaper like skin after a burn.

I walked barefoot across the soft, swollen floorboards to the front window. It was streaked with oil and dust, like everything else in the house, and I had to wipe it twice to even see through. The horizon was gray. Water met sky in a blur of static light. You couldn’t tell where the Gulf ended and the island began anymore. Everything bled together—salt, sky, concrete, rot. A seagull stood on a half-submerged mailbox, its feathers half-melted from whatever still leaked out of the refineries upriver. It watched me like it remembered how to hate.

From the next room, I heard our father. Praying. Loud, sharp syllables. Repeating the Creed. The proper one. The one from the Blue Book, not the public feed. He wanted to say it in the original protocol cadence, said the new versions had too much mercy baked in. Every morning, he broadcasted the prayer at full volume, even if the receiver was dead. Said it mattered to the air.

Voss would hear and answer.

Mae glanced at me through the mirror. Didn’t stop humming.

I sighed. Everyday began with a sign.

"You ready champ?" I asked her?

She always looked like she belonged somewhere better. Even here—barefoot in a flood-wrecked house, humming some dead man’s gospel with sea salt in her hair—Mae still had this way of being clean. Not polished. Not untouched. Just... unruined. Her eyes hadn’t gone dull like Mom’s. Her spine hadn’t curled under the weight of Dad’s sermons. She still believed the world might be good if you smiled first. And that scared the hell out of me. Because I knew what waited outside the island, outside even this house. I'd heard the stories, seen the refugees ferried in from the mainland with eyes gone wrong and Sparks twitching in their teeth. Even Dad—loyalist that he was—knew what would happen to a girl like Mae if the world got its hands on her. And I swore, right then, I’d keep that look in her eyes for as long as I fucking could.

"You bet, sport," Mae said back with a smile. Dumb names we called each other getting dumber.

Downstairs, our mother stood by the sink. Thin robe pulled tight around her, hair frizzed and limp. She wasn’t really looking outside, just staring through the smeared glass at the corpse of the ferris wheel two blocks over. Salt fog clung to it like gauze. She held a cigarette that wasn’t lit, just rolled between two fingers like she was waiting for permission.

“Is he gone yet?” she asked me when I entered the kitchen.

“He’s still praying," I told her.

"Fat lot of good that's ever done us."

I almost choked on her words, hoping Dad didn't hear them.

He stepped in then, boots clunking on the swollen floorboards.

Luckily, it didn't look like he'd heard her lack of faith.

Or else he'd have come in swinging.

His waders were patched with plastic tape, and the left one squelched when he moved. A SynRgy emblem, sun-faded but proud, was still stitched to his vest. He slapped a fishhook into the bandolier like it was a bullet. A rusted tackle box dangled from one hand, and the other held a cheap filtered net with algae already clinging to the mesh.

“Don’t be late,” he said. Not looking at us. "We're going to eat good tonight. I promise."

Mae nodded like a soldier. I didn't believe him, but nodded anyway.

He grabbed a dented bucket from the corner and stepped back outside. The door stuck on the frame. He kicked it until it groaned open and then was gone. Another day of broken promises and booze.

Mom lit her cigarette with a match she struck on the stove burner. She didn’t inhale, just held it to her lips and let it die slowly. The smell filled the room like guilt.

Mae and I sat at the table. One  bit of dog —leftovers from last night—and a SynRgy MRE. Unlabeled. Blocky. Just stamped: NUTRI-GEL 9-A. I peeled it open. The smell was copper and mud. We split it evenly. Fish for her. Gel for me. Neither of us chewed much.

Mae looked at me with her mouth full. “Tastes like moldy wires.”

“Means it’s fresh.”

We finished without speaking. Our hands brushed when we passed the tin back and forth. She smiled once. Just a flicker.

I stood to grab my bag—a mildewed satchel stitched with a cartoon patch that had long since faded—and Mae pulled on her thin canvas shoes, the soles flapping like open mouths.

Before we stepped out, I turned back.

Mom was sitting now. At the sink. Smoke curling around her eyes. Her hand shook as she reached for something behind the curtain—a little kit. Wired glass vial. Cracked eyejack.

“Don’t do it today," I asked, trying not to beg. "He'll be so mad if you do."

She froze.

“Please. Not today.”

She didn’t look at me. Just kept her eyes on the water outside.

“I won’t,” she whispered.

I didn't believe her. Hugged her anyway.

"Don't forget to bring home your MRE's," she said as I pulled away. "He'll be mad when he doesn't catch any fish again."

We left without another word. The tide was rising. The boards creaked again. Behind us, the Signal hummed from somewhere no one could see.



####

The water never receded, not really. It just shifted sides—took a different alley each morning, left new stains, lifted new corpses. Mae skipped around the worst puddles like it was a game. She said they looked like mirrors.

We moved down a path of rotting plywood and flattened sheet metal, strung between sunken curbs and collapsed porches. Duckboards, Dad called them, though I don’t think he remembered where the name came from. Rusted cars leaned out of the water like drowned beasts. One still blinked its hazards every few seconds—solar panel cracked but stubborn.

We passed the old tattoo shop. A SynRgy banner hung over the busted awning, half-burned but still legible: OBEDIENCE IS BLESSING. Someone had spray-painted over it in dripping red: VOSS IS A VIRUS. Another wall shouted: UNPLUG THE LIGHT. The 'U' had been replaced with a burning halo. That symbol showed up more and more lately. Even in chalk. Even on skin.

We didn’t talk. Mae hummed again. I kept my eyes on the street.

It was the water you didn’t see that got you. Bits of rebar, busted glass, jagged edges of god-knows-what just beneath the surface—waiting. You catch the wrong rust, the wrong cut, and tetanus would take you slow. Jaws locking, spine curling in on itself. No real med centers left out here. No antibiotics, unless you counted the mold some kid scraped from a storm drain and prayed over. Dad said Voss would decide if you lived or not. If you were worthy.

A checkpoint loomed ahead—four teens in composite armor stitched together from sports pads and riot gear. One wore a gas mask with no filter. Their SynRgy badges were hand-painted, proud and crooked. They weren’t official. Just local boys playing empire. One of them smoked a rolled cigarette down to the paper. His eyes locked on us.

“School’s that way?” one called. Not a question.

“Yeah,” I said.

“Got your proof?”

I pulled the slip from my pocket. Damp. Folded too many times. He didn’t read it. Just nodded and waved us through like it meant something.

One of the boys spat near Mae’s feet. I kept my mouth shut.

We walked faster after that. Passed the bar that never reopened. Passed a man on a crate selling soaked cigarettes and maybe pills. Passed the old carousel where the horses had all been stripped for firewood.

Then we saw him.

Slumped against a brick wall, mouth working like he was talking to ghosts. Fingers twitching in strange rhythms. Clothes tattered. Head shaved except for a burned streak where the band had been ripped away. He smelled like piss and ozone.

Sip streamer.

Mae tensed beside me.

He looked up—pupils blown wide, face sunburned to leather. One eye twitched to the beat of some song only he could hear.

“You got Signal?” he rasped. “I’ll trade blood. I’ll suck your dick.”

He grabbed my ankle. His hand was cold and dry, like a snake’s belly.

I kicked him off, harder than I meant to. He folded in on himself, laughing.

Then his head tilted.

"How about her, then?" he hissed. "What do you want for her? I’ll download her myself."

Mae shrank behind me. I didn’t say a word. Just stared him down until his eyes slid away, still grinning like he thought it was funny. Like he hadn’t just threatened to eat my sister’s soul.

His fingers twitched faster. The laughter bubbled up again, thin and sour.

We moved on.

We didn’t run. But we didn’t linger either.

I hated how close mom was to that man. How little space sat between her and the streamer we’d just passed. One more plug-in and she could gone too—mind cooked, soul frayed, skin twitching to some signal pumped through copper veins. Sip streaming didn’t just eat you. It hollowed you. Left you talking in tongues that weren’t yours, seeing dreams that didn’t belong to you. SynRgy pumped their digital Heaven full of poor folk like her. Like us. Voss called it transcendence. I called it cannibalism. Elites in Heaven High snorted our people like powdered gold. Got high on echoes of pain. But the worst part? We did it to ourselves. Lined up for the hit. Paid for the wire. Called it rapture. Mom used to say it helped her see the stars again.

Now she didn’t even look up.

Farther down the road, another streamer lay facedown in a gutter. No twitch. No song. Just a blackened wire still dangling from the busted band wrapped around his forehead.

We kept moving. Just another day in Drowned Galveston.

We were almost at the edge of school zone when we passed a wall with fresh paint: THEY LIE. YOU BURN. SAVE YOURSELF.



####

The school sat on the second floor of what used to be a law office. Probably. The plaque had long since rusted off, and no one cared what the building was before the water came. What mattered now was elevation. Second story meant dry—dry walls, dry desks, dry ceiling tiles that didn’t sag with mold or collapse mid-lesson.

Thirty kids, maybe less, packed into folding chairs and rusted office chairs. All children of loyalists. Not the rich ones—those had long since boarded ferries for the mainland and enrolled in full-signal academies where the light never went out. These were the ones too stubborn or too broken to leave. Kids with fathers like mine. Kids with mothers who liked to sip stream. We didn’t talk about it. We just showed up.

The first hour was sermon. Always was. A flickering flatscreen bolted to the drywall glitched out a pre-recorded message from Voss himself. Or someone wearing his voice. No one could tell anymore. His image ghosted every few seconds—part messiah, part static. He told us about duty. About sacrifice. About how the Spark must never be wasted on the unworthy. About how God had chosen SynRgy as the vessel for Earth’s last salvation.

“To the young men of the Signal,” Voss said, his voice like velvet pulled over rusted wire, "know that you are the hands of tomorrow’s order. You are strong. Capable. Designed for service—whether in stewardship, security, or spiritual transmission. You will be placed according to your aptitude, optimized to uplift the vessel of Earth through devotion."

He paused. Smiled.

“And to our precious young women—you are the sanctuaries. The keepers of warmth. You will nurture, instruct, and support the new world as mothers, as caretakers, as wives of worth. Your softness is not weakness—it is divine structure.”

His smile didn’t reach his eyes. I don’t think it ever had.

“Each of you has been assigned a role. Trust that the Signal knows better than doubt. Trust that Voss knows you.”

Then the screen flickered. His face pixelated for half a beat—just long enough for something else to bleed through. A shape, maybe. A child’s face. Gone in a blink.

The hymn resumed.

Nobody breathed.

No one interrupted. You had to sit through the whole thing to get your MRE. That was the rule. One sermon, one ration. Same thing every morning. Salvation through loyalty. Hunger through doubt.

Mae sat beside me, small hands folded, eyes distant. She didn’t blink when the screen flickered red and a compliance hymn droned through the speakers. Her stomach growled loud enough for me to hear it. I reached for her hand under the desk.

“Almost over,” I whispered.

She nodded. But didn’t smile.

I looked around. Most of the kids looked the same—hollow-eyed, waiting. One scratched at his wrist until it bled. Another had drawn the burning halo on the back of his hand with ink from a broken pen.

The girl in the front row rocked in time with the hymn, eyes wide like she was trying to force the Signal deeper into her skull.

This was school.

This was what was left of it.



####

We left the building single file, clutching our MREs like they might vanish. Mae’s grip was tight on hers, knuckles pale. Mine was already warm from the sun and the sweat in my palm. The packet buzzed faintly with heat, like it was trying to simulate food.

The alley behind the school had always been a risk. Not because of water or rot—but because everyone knew where the rations came from. Even the rebels. Especially the rebels.

They waited for us in the shade of a collapsed overhang—three of them, maybe four. Teenagers, older than me, skin sun-darkened and eyes too sharp. Not loyalist kids. No badges, no uniforms. Just threadbare shirts, boots held together with string and prayer, and teeth bared like dogs that hadn’t eaten in a week.

“Hey,” the tallest one said. “Look at that. Fresh meat and fresher lunch.”

Mae stepped behind me before I told her to. I tried to sidestep them, keep moving. I knew better than to make eye contact.

But the short one moved quick. Blocked our path. The other two closed in behind.

“Don’t be stupid,” I said. My voice cracked.

The tall one grinned. “We’re hungry too.”

"But we don't like praying to your false god."

One of them lunged. Grabbed at my arm, yanked at the MRE. I swung instinctively, caught him in the shoulder, but he didn’t even flinch. Just punched me in the ribs—hard.

I went down.

Mae screamed. I heard the rip of her MRE bag as another boy grabbed it. She kicked him. Hard. He cursed and slapped her.

I crawled back up, blood in my mouth. Grabbed a chunk of broken rebar from the ground. Swung wide. Missed. Someone kicked my knee. I crumpled again.

Then—

The air shattered.

A sound like the sky breaking open. The ground jumped. Windows blew inward. A pressure wave slammed us all sideways.

The building behind us—our school—bloomed into fire. Red, then white, then gone. Concrete cracked like old teeth. Glass and metal and bodies thrown like toys.

I couldn’t hear anything for a full ten seconds. Just a high whine in my skull. Dust choked the alley. Ash rained down.

The rebel kids bolted. Gone in a flash, vanishing into smoke.

Mae coughed beside me. Crying, maybe. I couldn’t tell.

I pulled her close. Held her tight. We stared at the flame where our classroom used to be. Where thirty kids used to sit. Where the screen flickered and Voss smiled and told us who we were.

Gone.

“They blew up the school," Mae said, matter-of-factly. 

I felt sick. My hands shook.

The thought burned through me like acid: If we’d still been in there, or if the bombers had better timing, we’d be dead. 

Five minutes was all the difference between life and death. 

They bombed a classroom.

Kids.

They said the rebels were fighting for freedom. For the future. For the broken and the lost.

But they set fire to children to make a point.

I didn’t know who to hate more—those kids who tried to steal our food, or the men who set the bomb. They were all Free States Rebels. 

My cheek throbbed. My chest ached.

The three rebel kids scattered. They already had our food. 

We walked home in silence. No one stopped us. No patrols. No questions. The smoke curled above us like a flag.



####

The house smelled like mildew and something sour when we stepped inside. The couch had sunk deeper into the warped floor, and Mom had sunk with it. One leg draped over the armrest, eyes half-lidded, mouth slack.

The sip-streamer band coiled from her temple like a parasite, the end jammed into a wall socket blackened around the edges. Her fingers twitched in slow, ugly patterns. Every few seconds her jaw would clench like she was chewing on a wire.

“Mom?” I said.

Nothing.

I knelt beside her, touched her arm. Her skin was cold with sweat. The eyejack blinked faint red, then pulsed. She started to murmur—low, wet sounds. Then words.

"So many voices. All at once... like... like screaming stars. They're in the Core. They’re inside the Core. Oh God, it’s beautiful. It hurts. It hurts so good."

She smiled, just barely. Teeth stained. “They’re all still in there, you know. All of them. Screaming. But alone. Together but alone. So loud it feels like love.”

“Mom, stop. You have to stop.”

I tried to pull the jack free. It sparked.

She flinched and snarled, then went limp.

“Shit,” I whispered. “Come on.”

Mae just stood near the table, clutching herself.

Then the door opened.

Boots. Wet. Stumbling.

Dad.

Empty bucket. No fish. Smell of booze and seaweed. Eyes like broken glass.

He froze when he saw her. When he saw the wire.

“What the fuck—” he whispered.

He didn’t ask questions after that.

The belt came off in one motion.

“Room. Now.”

Mae moved. I didn’t.

He advanced.

“I said get to your fucking room.”

“She’s sick. That’s all. She didn’t stream, I swear. She was just tired.”

“You calling me blind?”

“No, sir. But we lost our MREs. We got jumped. Rebels. They—”

He slapped me. The crack echoed.

“Fucking liar.”

“Dad—”

Another blow. Harder. My ear rang.

“Surrounded by fucking liars. You think you can just waltz in here, with no food, no faith, and feed me that bullshit?”

He turned to Mae, who had stopped in the hallway.

“Where’s your MRE? Did you listen to Lord Voss’s sermon or did you skip it too?”

Mae tried to speak, but nothing came.

“Goddamn parasites,” he growled. “You wanna rob your own family of salvation?”

“It was me,” I said. “Just me. She didn’t do anything. I dropped mine. The rebels took hers. Just me.”

He stared at me, breathing hard.

Then turned.

Grabbed Mom by the shoulder and yanked her upright. The jack popped loose and sparked against the outlet.

She moaned. Tried to speak.

He slapped her.

“You fucking cunt. You think this is a game? You think this is a temple? You’re the reason we’re still here. You’re the reason they didn’t take us north.”

Another slap. Her lip split.

“Broken fucking uterus. Can’t even give me another son. Can’t even cook. Can’t even stay awake.”

“Stop!” I shouted. “Please, stop!”

“Get. Out.”

He shoved me hard. I stumbled backward into Mae. We both fell.

“I’ll deal with you later,” he said.

His voice had gone calm again.

That scared me more than the shouting.

We crawled to our room.

Behind the thin wall, we heard the sound of him hitting her again.

Then silence.

And somewhere, beneath the floorboards, the house creaked with the tide.
